name: Tiered sensing cadence and data capture
consequences: Direct effect: default nightly low-burden monitoring reduces participant burden and hardware complexity while preserving continuous sensing. Downstream implications: enhanced PSG nights increase data richness and event detectability but raise staffing demands and costs, creating trade-offs between data quality and throughput.
options: Implement default nightly low-burden monitoring with dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors, reserving enhanced PSG for predefined escalation nights.
Establish a fully automated escalation workflow that triggers full PSG after any flagged event, with technician override as needed.
Transition to a dynamic, patient-driven escalation model that alternates between low-burden monitoring and full PSG based on interim scoring accuracy and event density.
review: Tiered sensing cadence vs data fidelity. Weakness: The options may understate the challenges of coordinating multiple sensor streams and ensuring timely annotation across variable stay lengths.

name: Recruitment and retention strategy
consequences: Direct effect: improved enrollment and event yield raise statistical power and reduce delays. Downstream: higher compensation and support costs create budget pressure and potential equity concerns, along with increased administrative coordination.
options: Increase per-night compensation to a higher level and introduce milestone-based top-ups for achieving target event yield.
Offer flexible stay windows with predefined extensions and robust participant support (transport, lodging) to optimize retention.
Tighten pre-screening and partner engagement to reduce early dropouts through enhanced privacy assurances and community liaison.
review: Recruitment and retention pressure vs cost containment. Weakness: The options may not fully address potential sample bias introduced by compensation changes.

name: Data governance and sharing framework
consequences: Direct effect: clearer data access, privacy protection, and compliance across multiple partners. Downstream: governance reviews and data curation requirements may slow release and add admin workload.
options: Implement a formal data governance framework with de-identification standards, access controls, and a public de-identified subset.
Forge data-use agreements with partner institutions to enable cross-site validation while preserving privacy.
Negotiate a data-sharing consortium with regional sleep centers to benchmark event-triage tools against external controls.
review: Data governance vs openness. Weakness: The options may introduce governance bottlenecks that slow timely data access.

name: Event triage development and validation
consequences: Direct effect: semi-automated triage reduces manual review time and speeds analysis. Downstream: ongoing calibration and data infrastructure upgrades are needed to sustain accuracy.
options: Develop a semi-automated event triage with a human-in-the-loop scoring system, using dual scorer agreement as the validation benchmark.
Adopt an iterative annotation pipeline with active learning to reduce manual review time and improve triage precision.
Pilot a vendor-neutral, open-source triage framework with standardized event categories to enable cross-site comparison.
review: Event triage development vs manual review burden. Weakness: Over-reliance on automated triage could miss rare events or degrade scorer reliability if not carefully maintained.

name: Site operations, safety, and staffing
consequences: Direct effect: improved safety and data throughput with robust coverage. Downstream: coordination complexity and potential cost implications from external providers.
options: Implement cross-trained staffing rotations to maintain 8-suite coverage with safety margins.
Deploy a formal escalation protocol for medical and safety incidents, with explicit handoff routines and drills.
Outsource non-core tasks (housekeeping, maintenance) to preserve research focus and improve schedule predictability.
review: Site operations vs staffing cost and safety. Weakness: The approaches may not scale evenly during extended stays or high-risk events.

name: External partnerships and funding strategy
consequences: Direct effect: diversifies funding streams and protects scientific independence. Downstream: potential conflicts of interest and longer funding cycles may slow decision-making.
options: Formalize pharma collaboration with pre-agreed publication rights and transparent conflict-of-interest management.
Pursue multi-source funding including national grants and private philanthropy to diversify risk.
Maintain strict independence by limiting industry engagement until after data maturity.
review: External partnerships vs scientific independence. Weakness: Some options may blur lines between academic goals and industry interests.

name: Sensing integration prioritization and data quality gating
consequences: Direct effect: Establishes a phased data-collection strategy that improves data fidelity by layering low-burden sensors with targeted PSG nights. Downstream implication: this increases per-participant cost and scheduling complexity, which may slow enrollment pace. Trade-off: higher data quality comes at the expense of operational burden.
options: Consolidate monitoring to a phased, multi-modal approach combining dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors, with escalation to full PSG only on flagged events.
Maintain full nocturnal PSG for all participants across the stay to maximize clinical resolution regardless of burden.
Adopt an event-driven escalation protocol where enhanced PSG nights are triggered by automated triage confidence above a threshold and manual review.
review: Core tension: Data fidelity from multi-modal sensing vs. participant burden and cost. Weakness: The options may undervalue the value of in-person PSG nights for rare events that automated triage might miss.

name: Participant recruitment and consent pathway optimization
consequences: Direct effect: Improves enrollment yield and cohort alignment by structured pre-screening and collateral history collection. Downstream implication: increases upfront administrative workload and requires expanded consent handling and data verification. Trade-off: faster recruitment may be compromised by additional checks, but data quality improves.
options: Implement a structured pre-screen protocol with collateral history checks to improve enrollment yield and reduce dropouts.
Broaden recruitment channels to regional sleep clinics and patient organizations to diversify the cohort.
Introduce flexible compensation and stay extensions to minimize attrition after the initial screening.
review: Core tension: Representativeness of the cohort vs. screening efficiency. Weakness: Broadening channels may dilute diagnostic homogeneity and complicate data harmonization.

name: Safety and incident response framework for residential setting
consequences: Direct effect: Establishes a 24/7 safety presence with defined escalation protocols and routine drills, improving response times during incidents. Downstream implication: raises staffing costs and requires governance structures to monitor safety metrics, which can affect study pacing.
options: Establish a 24/7 on-site safety presence with defined escalation protocols and regular drills to maintain a safe environment.
Institute remote monitoring for certain safety-critical alerts to reduce on-site staffing while preserving rapid response.
Create a formal incident review board with weekly safety metrics to drive continuous improvement.
review: Core tension: Safety coverage needs vs. on-site staffing cost and fatigue. Weakness: The options may understate the complexity of high-risk events requiring immediate in-person response.

name: Privacy-by-design data architecture
consequences: Direct effect: Implements role-based access controls and de-identification pipelines to protect participant privacy. Downstream implication: can slow data sharing across teams and institutions, increasing governance overhead and coordination needs.
options: Institute role-based access controls and de-identification pipelines with time-limited data sharing agreements.
Publish a detailed data dictionary and repository indexing to enable reproducibility while preserving privacy.
Engage regional ethics committees to review data-sharing plans for each cohort before any release.
review: Core tension: Privacy vs. data sharing and research collaboration. Weakness: The options may not fully address cross-institutional data transfer nuances.

name: Staffing model optimization under German regulatory constraints
consequences: Direct effect: Optimizes staffing by rotating night shifts and cross-training staff to ensure safety coverage while containing labor costs. Downstream implication: increases training requirements and can affect staff morale if schedules are tight; contingency plans needed for absences.
options: Adopt rotating night shifts with cross-trained technicians to ensure safety coverage while controlling fatigue and labor costs.
Create an on-call escalation pool to handle peak event loads without maintaining a large nightly staff.
Leverage automation-assisted annotation tools to reduce manual review time and free staff for complex cases.
review: Core tension: Safety coverage versus labor costs and staff well-being. Weakness: The options may compress staffing flexibility during unforeseen events.

name: External partnerships governance and publication alignment
consequences: Direct effect: Facilitates diversified funding and accelerates dissemination through partnerships with industry and academia. Downstream implication: increases the need for strict governance, conflict-of-interest management, and publication planning.
options: Negotiate industry partnerships with clear publication rights and data-sharing boundaries to align incentives.
Pursue academic-consortium collaborations to share methodology and co-author validations.
Establish phased collaborations with potential pharma partners while preserving independent analysis and publication timelines.
review: Core tension: Funding diversification vs independence of analysis. Weakness: Early-stage partnerships may constrain future data interpretations if not carefully structured.

name: Staged sleep-suite expansion aligned to data quality milestones
consequences: Expanding sleep-suite capacity directly increases participant throughput and data collection velocity, accelerating longitudinal phenotyping and method validation. A staged ramp improves safety oversight, staffing sufficiency, and data-management discipline; however, if expansion outpaces annotation throughput, data quality may suffer and review cycles may become strained.
options: Scale sleep-suite capacity only after achieving predefined annotation throughput and inter-rater reliability targets for clinical-grade data quality and safety assurances.
Implement a staged capacity plan that begins with eight active sleep suites and requires independent board approval before any expansion.
Prioritize optimization of existing suites through retrofits and safety refurbishments before pursuing new construction initiatives.
review: Expansion pace versus safety and data-quality governance. Weakness: Milestones may misalign with actual data throughput and staffing readiness, risking unsafe expansion.

name: Privacy-by-design bedroom video governance and consent framework
consequences: Implementing privacy-focused governance for bedroom video and sensor data preserves participant trust and regulatory compliance while enabling richer observational data. It also adds governance overhead, reduces immediate data availability for secondary analyses, and increases pre-screening and consent workflows, which may modestly lengthen enrollment timelines.
options: Institute opt-in, opt-out choices with role-based access to de-identified video transcripts and sensor streams for research staff.
Limit public sharing and implement de-identification pipelines before any data release to protect participant privacy.
Establish external privacy review focusing on video capture ethics and participant risk mitigation for ongoing studies.
review: Video data governance versus enrollment throughput. Weakness: Consent processes may become burdensome and reduce enrollment if overly granular.

name: Semi-automated event-triage workflow deployment and clinician validation loop
consequences: Deploying a semi-automated event-triage workflow lowers manual review burden and speeds scoring cycles, accelerating method validation and phenotyping. However, imperfect thresholds may increase false positives or negatives, necessitating periodic clinician validation and retraining.
options: Integrate real-time scoring and nurse-alert thresholds to trigger escalation nights with clinical-grade PSG on high-probability parasomnia events.
Calibrate triage algorithm to balance sensitivity and clinician workload by adjusting event-scoring thresholds through iterative reviews.
Extend triage to include cross-modal sensor evidence, such as mattress impulse data and infrared cues, to improve ranking.
review: Automated triage versus manual adjudication balance. Weakness: Over-reliance on automated scoring could degrade adjudication quality if not regularly recalibrated.

name: Regional recruitment channel expansion via Bonn-area sleep clinics and community neurologists
consequences: Diversifies participant pool and reduces screening delays, strengthening generalizability and statistical power for longitudinal analyses. It requires formal agreements, standardized pre-screening, and governance across sites, which may introduce operational friction and require additional coordination overhead.
options: Formalize referral agreements with regional sleep clinics to diversify participant sources and reduce screening time.
Offer flexible pre-admission pathways, including tele-screening and centralized intake, to widen eligibility while preserving safety standards.
Coordinate with patient associations to raise awareness and streamline consent processes for residential parasomnia studies.
review: Recruitment channels versus standardization of criteria. Weakness: External referrals may introduce site-to-site variability that affects enrollment quality.

name: Sensing technology diversification and non-contact modalities
consequences: Broadens data capture to reduce gaps during nocturnal events and improves robustness of event detection. It adds integration and maintenance tasks, increasing operational complexity and requiring more cross-disciplinary coordination.
options: Integrate non-contact thermal cameras and radar-based motion sensors to complement bed sensors without increasing burden.
Pilot wearable-free respiration and heart-rate monitoring through surface-contact sensors to reduce participant discomfort during overnight.
Validate redundancy across sensor modalities to improve data integrity during high-motion parasomnia episodes and reduce uncertainty.
review: Modality diversity versus data integration complexity. Weakness: Heterogeneous data streams may complicate synchronization and storage pipelines.

name: Operational safety and staffing ratios optimization under residential setting
consequences: Improved safety margins and staffing resilience reduce incident risk and clinician burnout while maintaining continuous operational coverage. However, higher staffing costs and scheduling rigidity may constrain expansion pace or overall budget, necessitating tighter financial governance.
options: Redesign night-shift rosters to maintain constant safety margins while preserving staff well-being and retention over long term.
Implement scheduled drills and rapid-response protocols to minimize response time during parasomnia events at night.
Establish independent safety audits and incident review cycles to address recurrent risk patterns across suites.
review: Safe operations versus budget pressure. Weakness: Elevated labor costs may limit scalability and require funding reprioritization.
